So code:

public int reverse(int x) {

    int sign = 1;

    if (x < 0) sign = -1;

    int num = x * sign; // absolute value

    int reversedNum = 0;

    while (num > 0) {

        int digit = num % 10;

        num = num / 10;

        // Check overflow before adding the digit

        if (reversedNum > Integer.MAX_VALUE / 10 || 

            (reversedNum == Integer.MAX_VALUE / 10 && 

             ((sign == 1 && digit > 7) || (sign == -1 && digit > 8)))) {

            return 0;

        }

        reversedNum = reversedNum * 10 + digit;

    }

    return reversedNum * sign;

}

Wait, but what about when x is 0? Well, the loop won't run, returns 0 * sign → 0. Which is correct.

Example 3: x = 120 → num 120 → digits 0, 2, 1. reversedNum becomes 0 → 0*10 + 2 = 2 → 2*10 +1=21. return 21 * 1 → 21.

public int reverse(int x) {

    int sign = 1;

    if (x < 0) sign = -1;

    int num = x * sign; // absolute value

    int reversedNum = 0;

    while (num > 0) {

        int digit = num % 10;

        num = num / 10;

        // Check overflow before adding the digit

        if (reversedNum > Integer.MAX_VALUE / 10 || 

            (reversedNum == Integer.MAX_VALUE / 10 && 

             ((sign == 1 && digit > 7) || (sign == -1 && digit > 8)))) {

            return 0;

        }

        reversedNum = reversedNum * 10 + digit;

    }

    return reversedNum * sign;

}

Wait, but what about when x is 0? Well, the loop won't run, returns 0 * sign → 0. Which is correct.
